entry.title: Re: How to Display Position Code in Latest People Profile Header – SAP SuccessFactors EC Workaround
entry.content:
Hi @rushdarizvie,

Good day!

As suggested by @fvt, one possible workaround is to create a new field (Ready Only) and configure an onView business rule so that only the required external code is displayed.

In addition, with the new 1H26 update, there is a new option called "Show External Codes" under Settings > People Profile Data Display. When this setting is enabled, external codes will be displayed on the latest People Profile. However, please note that this is currently an all-or-nothing setting, meaning all external codes will appear on the profile.

Thanks,
Arfat

entry.title: Introduction to Tendering(Sub-Contracting) in SAP Transportation Management
entry.content:
Introduction to Tendering in SAP TM 

Tendering refers to the subcontracting process in which a shipper or logistics service provider (LSP) offers a Freight Order (FO) or Freight Booking (FB) to one or more carriers and awaits their acceptance prior to awarding the business.
In SAP Transportation Management (TM), the carrier is defined as the business partner to whom the freight order is subcontracted. The executing carrier may differ; this distinction is relevant when the awarded carrier further subcontracts the transportation to a third party. This separation enables tracking of the contracted party versus the entity that physically moves the freight.
 
Tendering functions as the digital equivalent of a procurement cycle for freight, executed within the SAP TM document, and provides full traceability of all offers and responses.

Tendering in the end-to-end process flow

Tendering comes after planning/Optimization, when the FO or FB is built, and before execution(Refer Figure 1)

Figure 1

Now let’s focus on different process and steps in Tendering and see what actually happens inside it.

Tendering is a Six-Step Process, that takes a freight order,find the right carrier and updates the Freight order with Awarded Carrier

           

Step 1 Freight Order :  FO is created by the planner or automatically from freight units, with routes and stages defined, and the document is in the correct lifecycle status for tendering. No tendering actions occur at this stage; the FO serves as the input. Data integrity is critical, as all subsequent steps rely on the FO's information. Incorrect origin or destination details result in the wrong carrier pool at Step 2. Errors in weight, volume, or equipment requirements may exclude qualified carriers or include unqualified ones. Missing qualification flags, such as hazardous goods or temperature control, can lead to carriers accepting loads they cannot legally or operationally handle. The Freight Order is the source of truth. Any error at this stage will affect the entire tendering process.Incorrect data at Step 1 leads to an incorrect carrier pool at Step 2 and inaccurate rates at Step 3.

 
Step 2: Carrier Selection and Ranking. : Carriers are filtered based on eligibility criteria for the specified lane, mode, equipment, and operational constraints, then ranked by either transportation lane priority or carrier scorecard. The ranked list is used for the next tendering type, establishing the sequence for Peer-to-Peer tendering and defining the eligible pool for Broadcast
 
Step 3 : RFQ creation & publishing : At this stage, the freight order is sent to the carrier. SAP TM generates a formal Request for Quotation (RFQ) that includes pickup and delivery details, load specifications, special handling requirements, and the proposed rate. The RFQ is delivered as an email, an electronic EDI message, or a web portal notification (BN4L, CCP). The content remains the same; only the delivery channel varies. The recipient depends on the tendering type: one carrier at a time in Peer-to-Peer, all eligible carriers simultaneously in Broadcast, or any qualified carrier in the marketplace for Open/Spot.
 
Step 4 : Carrier quotation (FQ) : The carrier can accept, reject, or make a counteroffer. If the carrier accepts, the process is complete. If they reject, the tendering process starts again. If a counteroffer with a new price is allowed by the Tendering Profile, the process goes back to Step 5 for review.
 
Step 5 : Quotation evaluation & assignment : This explains how the award decision works. In Peer-to-Peer, the process is automatic and happens as soon as the first offer is accepted. For Broadcast, you can set it up so that either the first accepted bid wins or the best bid after the deadline is chosen. In Open or Spot, the process is usually manual, and a planner reviews all the bids in the Tendering Manager.
 
This 6-step overview also gives you a clear way to follow the rest of this blog series. Each type of tendering uses the same six steps, but Steps 3, 4, and 5 work quite differently:
 
StepPeer to PeerBroadcastOpen/Spot
3.RFQ Publishing 
Sent to carrier 1 only
Sent to all eligible at once
Published to B4NL
4.Carrier Quatation
Wait for carrier 1; if rejected, send to carrier 2
All carriers respond in parallel
Carriers self-select and bid
5.Evaluation&Assignment
First acceptance wins
First-accept or best-bid rule
Manual planner decision on bids
 
Steps 1, 2, and 6 are basically the same for all three types. The carrier selection logic and the FO update mechanics stay the same, while only the steps 3, 4, and 5 are different.
 
Understanding the difference in tendering between Basic and Advanced TM system
Before you start setting up tendering, make sure you know exactly what your TM license includes.
 
With Basic Shipping (Basic TM in S/4HANA), you can only confirm awards manually. There is no automated tendering engine, so the planner has to go through the subcontracting menu options step by step.
 
Advanced Transportation Management gives you a full automated tendering suite. This includes Peer-to-Peer, Broadcast, and Open (Spot) options, which can be sent directly by email or EDI, or through SAP SAP Business Network for Logistics (B4NL).
 
ScopeTendering CapabilityCommunication
Basic Shipping
Only manual award confirmation — planner sets statuses Manually: Send to Carrier → Set to Confirmed by Carrier
None automated
Advanced TM
Full automated tendering: Peer-to-Peer, Broadcast, Open / Spot
Direct (email / EDI) and via SAP B4NL
 
If you’re using Basic TM and your client wants automated tendering, you’ll need to discuss licensing rather than configuration.
 
Different Type of Tendering
SAP TM offers three types of tendering processes. Each type is designed to meet a specific business need.

Peer-to-Peer : system sends freight RFQs in sequence to the chosen carriers. There are two types: response required and no response required. If no acceptable quote is received, the process moves to the next step you have set up.
 
Broadcast tendering : system sends freight RFQs to all carriers at the same time. Depending on the settings, either the first acceptable quote wins, or after the response time ends, the system reviews all quotes and picks the lowest price.
 
Spot tendering : when a customer has yearly contracts with carriers but may also contact carriers without a contract.

A note on terminology: The standard term used in SAP TM is Open Tendering. The industry term Spot Tendering typically refers to the same concept — opening a load to the spot freight market, usually delivered through SAP B4NL Freight Collaboration. I'll use both terms throughout this series.

Status management during tendering 

Below is the Lifecycle,Subcontracting and Confimration Status in Freight order

Carrier communication channels

SAP TM can send a tender to a carrier using different channels. You can set up which channel to use for each carrier and business requiement
 

 

When SAP TM communicates a tender to a carrier, it can use any of the following channels. The choice is configured per carrier 

Behind the scenes, the integration service TM_ERPShipmentIntegration_In carries the Send Shipment and Notify of Tendering Result messages between SAP ERP and SAP TM — that's the technical glue that lets ERP know when a tendering result comes back.

 

Channel

Best for

Notes

SAP B4NL— Freight Collaboration

Modern S/4HANA TM deployments

Cloud-based, supports all three tendering types, carriers self-onboard

Email + PDF

Smaller carriers without IT integration

Low setup; carrier responds by clicking embedded reply link

EDI / IDoc

Mature LSP-carrier relationships

Bi-directional, fully automated

B2B XML / Web Service

Carriers with their own TMS

Real-time, programmatic

Carrier Collaboration Portal (CCP)

Older SAP TM 9.x landscapes

NetWeaver-based portal

Conclusion 

We have covered the basics of tendering in SAP Transportation Management: what it is, where it fits in the overall process, the six-step internal workflow, the difference between Basic and Advanced scope, the two main tendering types, the supported communication channels, and the TM business object model. With these key points in mind, you should now understand how SAP TM subcontracts a Freight Order to a carrier.
 

What's coming next in the series

Series

Topic

Focus

Series 2

Peer-to-Peer Tendering

Sequential carrier offers, priority logic, tendering profile config

**Version assumption: Configuration paths reflect S/4HANA TM (2021+). In SAP TM 9.x standalone, the customizing path sits under SCM Basis → Process Controller slightly different navigation, but conceptually the same objects.

entry.title: Transitioning from SAP Enable Now to the WalkMe Platform – A Brief Summary for SAP Enable Now Custom
entry.content:
For many SAP Enable Now customers, SAP Enable Now and SAP Companion have been critical components of their digital learning and in-application support strategies for many years. With the announcement of SAP Enable Now’s end of mainstream maintenance on November 30, 2030, it’s important that SAP Enable Now customers consider their approach to supporting business change and driving solution adoption, once Enable Now is no longer available.

This post introduces the solutions in the WalkMe platform, for existing SAP Enable Now customers, and provides important information about the transition timing and strategy.

What is the WalkMe platform?

WalkMe is a digital platform that helps organisations improve how people learn, adapt, and execute work across enterprise applications. The WalkMe platform consists of multiple solutions that allow customers to identify and resolve digital friction points in their system workflows and a single solution that supports digital learning.  WalkMe is a recognized global leader in this space.  Its solutions support outcomes such as faster onboarding, better process compliance, improved data quality, reduced support demand, and stronger return on software investments.

WalkMe systems are classified as either Digital Adoption or Digital Learning solutions.  Briefly, these solutions can be described as:

  1. WalkMe’s Digital Adoption solutions are designed to overlay enterprise applications, such as S/4HANA and SuccessFactors, to enhance the users’ experience of those applications.  Digital adoption solutions drive greater user productivity and capability by guiding users in application and automating common, repetitive tasks. 
  2. WalkMe’s AI-native Digital Learning solution was designed to improve the quality of training and ensure that critical content is always easily accessible by an organization’s employees.  The learning content can be distributed through a modern, searchable portal and is also available in application to allow easy, continual access by end users.

For SAP Enable Now customers, the WalkMe digital adoption solutions correspond to the solutions created and delivered using SAP Companion or SAP Companion for Desktop, e.g. Guided Tours and in-application message bubbles.  WalkMe’s digital learning solution corresponds to the solutions created in Enable Now Producer, including books, book pages, and simulations.

The WalkMe Digital Adoption solutions

WalkMe licenses its digital adoption solutions in two ways.  Digital adoption capability can be licensed for a single enterprise application, such as S/4HANA, or for an unlimited number of applications within an organization.  WalkMe solutions licensed for a single application are referred to as WalkMe Premium, e.g. WalkMe Premium for S/4HANA or WalkMe Premium for SuccessFactors.  When organizations license the WalkMe digital adoption capabilities for an unlimited number of applications, the solution is referred to as WalkMe DAP.

The WalkMe Premium and WalkMe DAP solutions offer comprehensive digital adoption capabilities for organizations that are undergoing significant change or plan to undertake major business transformations. These solutions allow organizations to understand actual user behavior, to build solutions to resolve friction points in complex user workflows, and to track and report changes in user efficiency and productivity. The WalkMe digital adoption capabilities are particularly valuable for businesses that are introducing new platforms, redesigning processes, or trying to increase value from complex application landscapes that span SAP and non-SAP systems.

Common business issues addressed by the WalkMe Premium and WalkMe DAP solutions include slow adoption of new technology, inconsistent process execution, underused software features, low employee productivity, and fragmented user experiences across systems.

WalkMe Premium and DAP solutions can be used to support SAP and non-SAP applications.  They can also be used to support web applications, such as S/4HANA, desktop applications, such as SAP GUI, and mobile applications.

WalkMe digital adoption solutions can be licensed to support a customer’s employees or a customer’s customers.  Examples of solutions used to support employee system usage are WalkMe Premium for S/4HANA and WalkMe Premium for Ariba.  The WalkMe solution used to support a customer’s customers is called WalkMe Premium for Customers.

The SAP Enable Now equivalent of the WalkMe digital adoption solutions is SAP Companion for web applications and SAP Companion for Desktop for desktop applications.  Think of the WalkMe solutions as SAP Companion on steroids.  There is definitely overlapping capabilities of the WalkMe solutions and SAP Companion but also capability available in WalkMe that cannot be replicated with SAP Companion, e.g. understanding actual user behavior to identify frictions in user workflows.

Figure 1 WalkMe Digital Adoption capabilities

 

 

 

 

 

 

The WalkMe Digital Learning solution

WalkMe Learning Arc is the WalkMe solution most directly aligned to the digital learning use cases historically addressed by SAP Enable Now, e.g. books, book pages, simulations and quizzes.  In WalkMe Learning Arc, content can be delivered as individual microlearning objects, including simulations, quizzes and screen recordings, or it can be structured into courses for delivery to end users.

WalkMe Learning Arc courses can be generated using AI.  The generated content can then be modified and additional content added by authors before being distributed to end users.  This approach to content creation is a significant enhancement on existing Enable Now capabilities.

WalkMe Learning Arc content can be delivered through a searchable company portal that contains the ability for users to quickly locate and access relevant content.  WalkMe Learning Arc content, such as simulations, can also be delivered in-application to ensure that it is available for end users as they work in the corresponding applications.

Figure 2 WalkMe Learning Arc AI course creation capability

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Retaining access to Enable Now Digital Learning content

When considering a transition to the WalkMe platform, it’s important that existing Enable Now Cloud customers determine if they wish to retain access to existing Enable Now digital learning content, such as books, book pages and simulations.  Customers that have invested time, effort and money into creating existing Enable Now digital learning content can continue to leverage that content by loading it onto the WalkMe Learning Arc platform.  Once loaded onto the WalkMe Learning Arc platform, the Enable Now content can be published and distributed to WalkMe Learning Arc end users, just like native WalkMe Learning Arc content objects.

Customers that load their existing Enable Now digital learning content onto the WalkMe Learning Arc platform can continue to update and supplement that content through an integration between WalkMe Learning Arc and Producer.  Enable Now content hosted on the WalkMe Learning Arc platform can continue to be updated, using the Producer integration, until November 30, 2030; even after a customer’s SAP Enable Now subscription has expired.  Following the November 30, 2030 end of life of Enable Now, the Producer integration will no longer be available but customers can continue to distribute, but not update, their Enable Now content through WalkMe Learning Arc.

If Enable Now customers wish to load their existing content onto the WalkMe Learning Arc platform, it’s critical that they license Enable Now and WalkMe Learning Arc simultaneously, i.e. both products must be licensed at the same time.  This is necessary to allow the Enable Now content to be loaded onto the WalkMe Learning Arc platform.  Once customers have loaded their Enable Now content onto the platform and tested that content, they can then allow their Enable Now licenses to expire, while still retaining access to the Producer integration.

Given that customers must subscribe to Enable Now and WalkMe Learning Arc simultaneously, to allow existing content to be loaded onto the WalkMe Learning Arc platform, a specific WalkMe Test and Go Live License, at a significantly reduced cost, is available to allow customers to perform the content transition and testing activities.  Please discuss this license with your SAP or WalkMe sales representative.

Figure 3 Retaining access to SAP Enable Now content after transitioning to WalkMe

 

 

 

 

 

When is the right time to transition to the WalkMe platform?

For many existing SAP Enable Now Cloud customers, November 30, 2030 seems a long way off, so no urgency to make a decision about the transition to WalkMe, right?  Unfortunately, April 30, 2027 is the actual date that most Enable Now customers should be considering when thinking about a WalkMe transition, not November 30, 2030.

While the SAP Enable Now Cloud solution will be available and supported until November 30, 2030, for customers with valid subscriptions, renewals of active subscriptions will not be possible beyond April 30, 2027.  This means that for SAP Enable Now Cloud customers, your Enable Now Cloud subscription, with an end date post April 30, 2027, will be Terminated on the end date of your contract.

Assuming no change to the April 30, 2027 final renewal date, one of three scenarios applies to existing Enable Now customers:

  1. Existing SAP Enable Now Cloud customers with a renewal date prior to April 30, 2027 have one final opportunity to renew their Enable Now Cloud subscription. 

    Customers that choose to renew their SAP Enable Now Cloud subscription should ensure that they agree on a contract end date that allows sufficient time to consider their future digital adoption and learning strategies and to transition to a new platform.  No further renewals of Enable Now Cloud are possible, beyond April 30, 2027.

    Customers with a renewal date prior to April 30, 2027 that have decided to transition to the WalkMe platform immediately, rather than renewing their Enable Now Cloud subscription, should consider the information provided above when determining their transition strategy and the WalkMe solutions that they plan to license.  Please allow sufficient time to transition, test, and deploy your existing Enable Now content on the WalkMe Learning Arc platform, before your Enable Now license expires.  Consider obtaining support from SAP, WalkMe, or a WalkMe partner if capacity constraints are likely to impact your ability to transition to WalkMe Learning Arc on your preferred timetable.

  2. Existing SAP Enable Now customers that do not have a renewal due prior to April 30, 2027, and who wish to extend their current contract end date, should contact their SAP CSM or sales representative to discuss the options for an early renewal.

    SAP Enable Now Cloud customers with contracts that end after April 30, 2027, and who do not plan to extend their current Enable Now Cloud contract, should ensure that they transition to their new platform prior to the end of their current Enable Now contract.

  3. SAP Enable Now Cloud customers with existing contracts who are interested in moving to the WalkMe platform prior to the end of their current Enable Now contract should discuss their transition options with their SAP CSM or sales representative.  These resources will be able to provide early transition options and additional insights to allow you to make the right decision for your organization.

Conclusion

The practical message is clear: SAP Enable Now customers should start thinking about their transition to WalkMe now.  Enable Now remains available and supported until November 30, 2030 for customers that are licensed; however, the final opportunity to renew your existing Enable Now Cloud licences is April 30, 2027.

Customers that wish to deploy in-application solutions that guide users and automate common, tedious tasks should consider relevant WalkMe Premium or DAP solutions.  These solutions allow customers to identify major frictions in their online workflows, and to develop and deploy solutions that resolve those frictions.

WalkMe Learning Arc allows customers to rapidly develop, in some instances using AI, and deploy learning content, including in application to ensure that employees can access the content continually as they work.  WalkMe Learning Arc provides the capability to continually reinforce the critical knowledge and skills that are essential to operating in a modern work environment.

Existing Enable Now customers must license WalkMe Learning Arc if they wish to retain access to their existing SAP Enable Now digital learning content, including simulations.  Customers with overlapping subscriptions of WalkMe Learning Arc and Enable Now Cloud can load their existing Enable Now content onto the WalkMe platform and distribute it to WalkMe Learning Arc end users.  They can also update their Enable Now content using an integration to Producer, launched from WalkMe Learning Arc.

Please contact your SAP CSM or sales representative with any further questions on this topic.  Good luck with your WalkMe transition and see you on the other side the fence!

entry.title: Looking for standard APIs for SAP Warehouse Management (WM) processes
entry.content:
Hi Team,

I am currently working on an integration project involving SAP Warehouse Management (WM) and need to trigger core warehouse processes via APIs.

Specifically, I am looking for standard APIs to handle the following requirements:

  • Create Transfer Order (similar to transaction LT01): Creating a TO for a specific material and quantity.

  • Transfer Stock / Move Storage Unit (similar to transaction LT10): Moving stock from one storage bin/type to another.

Any documentation, function modules, or best practices you can share would be greatly appreciated!

Thanks in advance,

Leelamohan

entry.title: The Procurement Monthly - May 2026
entry.content:
Summary

A couple of themes emerge from this months collection:

AI and automation are shifting effort away from transactional work toward category strategy, supplier collaboration, and disruption response, but only where data quality and operating models keep up.

The organisations that grow fastest use procurement as a growth engine, not just a savings function, and they track ROI, influence, and skills accordingly.

Cost and risk are not separate agendas, they are a singular conversation.

Cost pressure is structural, not cyclical, with energy, freight, labour, and tariffs combining to push total supply chain costs 5–7% above inflation through 2026, leaving many businesses facing more than 10% total cost exposure. At the same time, survey data from CIPS, QIMA, Hackett, CIO reports and Gartner all tell the same story: you are moving from visibility to execution, from pilots to practical AI. Gartner now expects 60% of supply chain disruptions to be resolved without human intervention by 2031, which will only work if procurement, IT and commercial teams fix data foundations, governance, and operating models first.

Kearney Supply Chain Navigator

Kearney’s Supply Chain Navigator suggests supply chain unit costs will rise 5–7% above inflation through the end of 2026, driven by Middle East energy disruption and shifts in US tariff policy. Disruption in the Strait of Hormuz is feeding through into fuel, petrochemicals, fertilisers and metals, raising many input categories 10–25% and hitting supply tiers that many procurement teams do not see directly. Shipping lanes are rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope, adding 10–14 days to voyages and pushing freight rates 30–70% higher on affected routes, with full normalisation expected to take six to twelve months after de‑escalation.

Tariff policy compounds the story. The key point is timing. The report shows cost shocks front‑loaded into the next two quarters; companies with thin inventory or short contracts feel the impact first. Leaders are mapping exposure to tariffs and Middle East routes, securing capacity, and building scenario‑based planning and dual‑sourcing, while laggards wait for the bill to arrive.

Global State of Procurement and Supply 2026 (CIPS / GEP)

The CIPS / GEP survey paints a profession with more influence and bigger expectations. Around 77% of functions operate in a centre‑led model, and a growing share of procurement leaders report directly to the CEO or executive committee, with finance now the minority reporting line. Teams manage a large share of spend, with two‑thirds responsible for at least 60% of direct goods and almost a third managing over 90%. Investment priorities centre on digital technology, staff development and AI, although AI expectations have become more cautious, with fewer respondents expecting dramatic productivity gains in the next two years.

Skills data shows demand for new technology skills, risk management and negotiation at the top of development priorities, while interest in leadership and influencing has dipped, especially in larger organisations. Cybersecurity is another fast‑rising theme, with two‑thirds of organisations updating procurement policies in response to cyber risk and almost 30% reporting a cyberattack in the past 12 months.

QIMA Global Sourcing Survey 2026

QIMA’s survey of more than 1,000 companies shows that after a decade of disruption, supply chains are better prepared, but 2026 still looks tough. Around four in five businesses expect costs to be a major disruption, with disruptive potential of cost factors almost doubling since 2024; raw materials and shipping lead, and labour costs are close behind. Tariffs hit US and China‑based supply chains hardest in 2025, with many shifting production to Vietnam, India or Mexico rather than reshoring.

Diversification, visibility and digitisation stand out as the three main response levers. About 43% of supply chains changed sourcing geography in 2025, and diversified supply chains were more likely to grow volumes than those that stood still. The average company now knows about 60% of its suppliers by name and country, but fewer than one in five has full visibility; the more mapped the network, the fewer issues with quality, compliance, capacity and schedule. Digitisation continues in a selective, budget‑conscious way, with three‑quarters of respondents planning further investment and clear benefits in communication, traceability and safety compliance for those with higher digital maturity.

Autonomous supply chains and disruption resolution - Gartner

Gartner forecasts that by 2031, 60% of supply chain disruptions will be resolved without human intervention, as AI tools sense, decide and act across supply networks. Drivers include geopolitical shocks, tariff churn and increasing frequency of events that outstrip human monitoring capacity. The projection assumes widespread deployment of agentic AI in control towers, planning, logistics and sourcing, tied deeply into transactional systems and risk data.

State of the CIO - Foundry

The CIO report points to a sharp focus on AI, cybersecurity and data platforms, with many CIOs reallocating budget toward AI agents, automation, and unified data architectures that support cross‑functional use cases including procurement and supply chain. Business and IT leaders increasingly expect procurement to plug into the same digital backbone as sales, marketing and operations, not run separate tools and data stores.

AI in Procurement – ProcureaAI

The whitepaper states that AI value in procurement is constrained more by fragmented data than by algorithms. It recommends a practical data strategy that consolidates spend, contract, supplier and risk data, defines clear ownership and governance, and establishes reliable pipelines into AI tools. The message here is simple: if you want autonomous sourcing, disruption resolution and better commercial insight, you need to fund data engineering and governance with the same seriousness as category strategies or sourcing events

Economist Enterprise – Procurement at a crossroads

Procurement is navigating a sharp return to basics — but with a heavier burden than before. Based on a global survey of 2,648 C-suite executives across 23 countries, the report finds that cost control has re-emerged as the primary measure of procurement's value, cited by 54% of executives (up from 43% in 2025), with CPOs and CSCOs rating it even higher at 58–59%. Yet the function is simultaneously expected to deliver on the expanded mandate it accumulated post-pandemic: risk management, supply-chain resilience, ESG compliance, and category management sophistication. This dual pressure — reduce costs and manage complexity — is playing out against a backdrop of largely flat headcount and declining executive confidence in procurement's effectiveness across almost every measured dimension

60% of executives naming digital transformation as their top priority (up from 38% in 2025) and 56% specifically targeting agentic AI for implementation or evaluation in the next 18 months. Yet the reality of deployment remains modest — only 23% of companies are scaling agentic AI solutions, and the biggest barrier is not technology itself but the foundations around it: fragmented data, poor systems integration (only 18% of leaders say their tech stack is fully integrated), and unclear ROI. Critically, 56% of executives report no improvement in decision-making through AI over the past 18 months, and just 9% want AI to lead most procurement decisions within three years — signalling that the human-AI balance firmly favours humans, with AI earning its keep in process automation and analytical support rather than strategic autonomy.

Procurement's boardroom influence — so hard-won during the pandemic years — is visibly receding: confidence in procurement's contribution to corporate strategy fell from 90% to 74% year-on-year, and CPO influence over digital transformation strategy dropped from 91% to 68%. The report's central message is clear: procurement leaders must now prove their value not by arguing for a broader mandate, but by delivering consistently across it — demonstrating measurable outcomes at a moment when scrutiny of returns has never been higher.

 

As always reach out to discuss more, and always happy to hear your thoughts!

entry.title: Re: Managing Invoices with SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (now SAP Ariba Invoicing)
entry.content:
Create Supplier Invoices with Document Information Extraction (Inbound Service API)

This inbound service provides functionality for importing supplier invoices directly to SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management using documents sent to the API from which information will be automatically extracted. For example, this allows to import multiple invoice files (such as PDF, JPEG, PNG and TIFF files) directly from an email to SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management.

Supplier Invoices with Document Information Extraction - /api/cim-create-invoice/v1/

entry.title: Unable to Find “Company Code Settings – Asset Accounting-Specific” in SAP Public Cloud
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support Team,

We are currently working on Asset Accounting configuration in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

However, we are unable to locate the configuration step “Company Code Settings – Asset Accounting-Specific” in the system.

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Application studio for Github copilot do...
entry.content:
Hi,
After opening the chat in your SAP Business Application Studio dev space, you'll see a message suggesting you to either login with GitHub credentials or select a model using "bring your own key" approach.
In your case, you can login with your GitHub enterprise user which includes the appropriate license.

In case you missed the message, you can also open the model selection drop-down below the chat prompt box, and click on the "GitHub Copilot" link.

See more information in the official SAP Business Application Studio documentation: https://help.sap.com/docs/bas/sap-business-application-studio/ai-chat

Thanks,
Rotem

entry.title: Re: Looking for standard APIs for SAP Warehouse Managemen...
entry.content: The question is about LE-WM (transfer order creation) not about EWM (warehouse order and task). As far as I know, LE-WM doesn't provide APIs.

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
Re: Looking for standard APIs for SAP Warehouse Managemen...
entry.content:
Hello @Leelamohan 

As far as I know LE-WM doesn't provide APIs, but it provides nice interface function modules. Why don't you use L_TO_CREATE_SINGLE for LT01 and L_TO_CREATE_MOVE_SU for LT10. Both functions are RFC enabled so you can easily expose them as web services.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: tax data is missing
entry.content: I found that when a purchase order is generated and there are two different warehouses in this purchase order, the system will automatically split this purchase order into two new purchase orders, namely Purchase Order -1 and Purchase Order -2. If some products in Purchase Order -1 are delivered and you modify the quantity or price of any product in Purchase Order -1, an error will be reported. It indicates that tax data is missing. However, after testing, I found that this situation does not occur when the data such as baseentry and base type of purchase order -1 is empty. Only by deleting and re-adding the product lines in purchase Order -1 can these base data be cleared. Are there any other ways to solve it?

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Event Routing per Domain with Metadata-Driven Consumer Orchestration
entry.content:
"One adapter. One router. Value Mapping entries. The domain decides consumption. Metadata decides behavior."

Introduction

After publishing the previous Kafka Topic routing blog , an interesting architectural question was raised via LinkedIn DM:

"How can SAP CPI control single consumers, multiple consumers, or both simultaneously for the same Kafka topic while still reading the topic with wildcard through only one Kafka Sender Adapter and one inbound channel?"

That question exposed a real gap.

The previous implementation demonstrated immutable routing per domain, but not dynamic consumer orchestration.

This blog introduces a metadata-driven Smart Router capable of resolving consumer behavior at runtime using Value Mapping and Groovy logic.

The result:

One Kafka topic per Domain/subdomain
→ One immutable router per domain/subdomain
→ N consumers resolved dynamically via metadata

without changing the producer, without changing the adapter, without redeploying the router.

Transport-Agnostic Routing Principle

Although this implementation uses the SAP Kafka Sender Adapter, the routing pattern itself is transport-agnostic.

Modern brokers like Solace (SAP AEM), RabbitMQ, or Azure Service Bus support native topic wildcards (*, >), which means a single inbound adapter can capture a wide stream of domain events. In those cases, the value of the Smart Router increases — because the alternative is polluting the iFlow with endless native Content-Based Routers.

The pattern depends only on:

  • an inbound event identifier
  • semantic metadata
  • consumer resolution logic

So the same model applies to:

  • SAP Event Mesh (EM/AEM), Solace, RabbitMQ
  • AMQP, MQTT, HTTPS/Webhooks
  • Event-driven REST endpoints

The transport changes. The routing principle does not:

Inbound Event

→ Metadata Resolution
→ Consumer Strategy
→ Domain Processing

However, the architectural pattern itself is not tied to Kafka.

⚠️This is a reference template, not a production-ready blueprint.

The principle is the deliverable. The implementation is your decision.

Scope and Governance Notice

This pattern follows SDIA / ODCP / EDCP semantic naming conventions. If your organization uses different standards, adapt topics, queues, packages, iFlows, Value Mappings, ProcessDirect URLs, and credentials accordingly. The routing logic itself is naming-independent.

Schema modeling, retries, DLQ, security hardening, mappings, and operational guardrails were intentionally simplified — this is a minimal proof of concept demonstrating immutable event routing.

This blog is the continuation of:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI

Reference blog for IDOC:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice

Reference blog for APIM:

SAP BTP - API Management with SDIA — GDCR & DDCR in Practice

SAP BTP - APIM - DDCR - Deterministic Routing Mechanism JavaScript Policy full explanation each step

The Real Architectural Problem

Traditional multi-consumer implementations usually introduce:

  • duplicated iFlows
  • additional adapters
  • hardcoded routing
  • custom producer headers
  • JMS orchestration
  • CPI-side retry logic

As a result, the producer starts knowing too much about downstream processing.

Kafka was designed to decouple producers from consumers. The producer should emit an event; the domain decides how the event is consumed.

Why custom headers and kafka.PARTITION were rejected

Headers such as x-routing-mode, x-consumer-count, or x-target-system would force the producer to know downstream orchestration details, violating producer–consumer decoupling.

kafka.PARTITION was also rejected because partition metadata is infrastructure-related, not semantic. Partitions may change due to rebalance, scaling, retention, or topic redesign. They cannot be used as a stable routing contract.

This implementation therefore uses:

Topic → Value Mapping → Consumer Strategy

The topic represents business intent. The metadata defines consumer behavior.

That is why the routing responsibility belongs to the domain — not to the producer.

Scalability: Why Centralizing the Inbound Matters

The Smart Router pattern does not eliminate processing iFlows. It eliminates duplicated inbound infrastructure.

In a domain-centric SDIA architecture, processing iFlows still exist because business responsibilities remain separated by domain behavior. That is governance — not sprawl.

Realistic example — Topics

Example - Topics:

  • acme.sales.o2c.order.created
  • acme.sales.o2c.order.confirmed
  • acme.sales.o2c.order.cancelled
  • acme.sales.o2c.invoice.created
 Traditional approach Smart Router
Kafka Sender Adapters41
Credentials41
Inbound iFlows41 immutable router
Processing iFlows44
Routing logicIflow spaguette with local Routing based content.metadata-driven
New consumernew iFlow + deployedit one VM row

The key distinction:

  • Processing separation = governance
  • Inbound adapter duplication = operational sprawl

The gain is not reducing all iFlows to one. The gain is freezing the inbound footprint while letting downstream processing evolve independently through metadata.

As new consumers appear, no new inbound adapters or router iFlows are required.

Only metadata evolves.

That reduces:

  • adapter proliferation
  • duplicated orchestration
  • deployment overhead
  • routing maintenance
  • credential rotation
  • operational TCO

while preserving domain ownership and decoupling.

The first model demonstrates the traditional integration approach: one adapter, one iFlow or consumer combination.

As event consumption grows, the landscape quickly evolves into duplicated orchestration, fragmented governance, and operational sprawl.

The second model centralizes routing responsibility inside a single immutable Smart Router per domain. Consumer behavior becomes metadata-driven instead of hardcoded, reducing adapter proliferation while preserving domain ownership and decoupling.

The Three Architectural Possible Models

Before implementing the solution, three architectures were designed and validated.

Only one became the recommended model.

Architecture 1 — Two Layers: Simple Router + Consumer Dispatcher

 

LayerResponsibility
Kafka AdapterReads topics via wildcard
Router iFlowResolves metadata existence
Dispatcher iFlowDecides single / multi / both / fallback
Processing iFlowsBusiness processing per consumer
  • Pros: clean responsibility separation, isolated dispatcher. 
  • Cons: extra ProcessDirect hop, more artifacts, higher latency.

Elegant, but increases artifact proliferation.

Architecture 2 — Single Layer: All-in-One Smart Router ✅ Recommended

 

LayerResponsibility
Kafka AdapterReads domain topics via wildcard
Immutable Smart RouterMetadata resolution + consumer strategy + dispatch
Processing iFlowsDomain business processing
  • Pros: fewer artifacts, simpler governance, immutable router, metadata-driven. 
  • Cons: router becomes denser; multicast/splitter orchestration is centralized.

The complexity becomes centralized rather than distributed across duplicated iFlows. 

This is the architecture implemented in this blog.

Architecture 3 — Three Layers: Global Event Router + Decision Layer ❌ Not Recommended as Default

A single router reading all topics from all domains was tested and deliberately excluded:

  • single point of impact for every domain
  • shared dependency violates SDIA domain autonomy
  • additional hops and latency
  • changes in the global router affect everything

The boundary belongs to the domain. The router must live inside it.

Trade-offs of This Pattern

To be honest about what you're adopting:

  • The router becomes denser — debugging multicast/splitter paths requires familiarity with the engine.
  • The pattern depends on semantic discipline in the Value Mapping. Sloppy metadata = sloppy routing.
  • Governance only holds if the team respects the domain boundary. The framework formalizes it; humans still have to honor it.
  • Validation of physical ProcessDirect endpoints is delegated to CPI runtime — a misconfigured VM entry is detected at execution, not at deploy.

Why the Router Owns Consumer Orchestration

The SAP Kafka Sender Adapter does not provide dynamic consumer orchestration. The router absorbs:

  • metadata resolution
  • consumer strategy
  • dispatch orchestration

Business processing remains inside the processing iFlows. The router decides how an event is consumed; the domain iFlows decide what the event means.

Why two Value Mapping contracts

The implementation separates:

  • Single Consumer Mapping
  • Multiple Consumer Mapping

Single routing is deterministic; multi routing requires iteration; “both” requires multicast. Splitting the contracts keeps the orchestration logic and the metadata readable.

Kafka retry behavior — NACK by design

Iflow ResultKafka Behavior
SuccessOffset committed
Error End EventOffset retained
 

FALLBACK intentionally raises an Error End Event, so Kafka retries the message later. Kafka already acts as the retry engine — adding JMS or Data Store retry loops would duplicate a responsibility Kafka already solves natively.

Allowed Headers — Critical Configuration

Before deploying, add the following to the Kafka Sender Adapter Allowed Headers field:

kafka.TOPIC

Without this, the kafka.TOPIC header is not propagated to the iFlow and the router receives an empty topic key — every message falls to FALLBACK.

You can also expose for observability (optional):

kafka.TOPIC, kafka.PARTITION, kafka.OFFSET or kafka.*

The Event Routing iFlow — Architecture 2 in CI

This implementation centralizes metadata resolution, consumer orchestration, and dispatching inside a single immutable Smart Router per domain, keeping the inbound layer stable while allowing downstream consumers to evolve independently through metadata.

Flow steps

  1. Kafka Sender — inbound Kafka Sender Adapter, wildcard topic, single channel.

  2. Groovy — Metadata lookup — resolves the topic against the Value Mapping (single + multi consumer contracts) and dynamically builds the ProcessDirect targets; preserves the original AVRO payload as Base64.

  3. Router Guard the entrance of DOMAIN LAYER  — evaluates is_mapped. If no mapping is found → Fallback branch.

  4. Router — One Consumer or XConsumer — evaluates is_single and is_xconsumer and selects the routing state: SINGLE, XCONS, BOTH, or FALLBACK.

  5. Single branch — Restore AVRO payload — Groovy restores the original Kafka AVRO payload before dispatching.

  6. Multiple Consumers branch — Build target channels — sets the target_channel property and prepares the multi-target list for iteration.

  7. Both branch — Parallel Multicast — fans out the message in parallel to the SINGLE path and the XCONS path simultaneously.

  8. Splitter — Multiple consumers from same topic — iterates over the resolved target channels (one dispatch per consumer).

  9. Restore payload + End — each branch restores the original AVRO payload before reaching its End event, ensuring multicast/splitter consistency.

  10. ProcessDirect Receiver — all branches dispatch to the corresponding domain processing iFlows via ProcessDirect.

Fallback path

  • Fallback — No Routing found — when neither contract resolves, the Groovy step Build exception response generates a structured JSON error and terminates at Error End 2 (offset is not committed → Kafka retries).

The Processing Iflow Topic — Architecture 2 in CI

After the Smart Router dispatches the event via ProcessDirect, the processing iFlow consumes the frozen AVRO payload, applies the schema contract, and converts it into business-readable XML or JSON.

  1.  Avro Schema (Content Modifier)
    • The AVRO schema is loaded as an Externalized Parameter directly into the iFlow, avoiding external lookups and keeping the processing flow immutable and lightweight.
  2.  Groovy: AVRO → JSON/XML
    • The Groovy script reads the binary AVRO payload, applies the schema contract, and converts the event into JSON or XML.

⚠️This is a reference template only. Schemas, mappings, output formats, and processing logic must be adapted to your own integration landscape.

Why the AVRO Schema Uses Externalized Parameters

The AVRO schema was intentionally stored as an Externalized Parameter via Content Modifier instead of Partner Directory, Data Store, or JMS.

Why ?

  • Partner Directory → designed for partner/pipeline configuration, not domain event contracts
  • Data Store → unnecessary database I/O for static schema reads
  • JMS → overlaps with Kafka buffering responsibilities

Keeping the schema as an Externalized Parameter, the iFlow and Groovy logic remain immutable while avoiding external lookups, hidden dependencies, and additional runtime overhead.

This pattern is optimized for small to medium schemas.

For enterprise-scale schema lifecycle governance and compatibility management, a dedicated Schema Registry remains the recommended approach.

The Architectural Trade-Off: High-Throughput Memory Footprint

While Base64 guarantees total payload protection, enterprise architects must account for underlying JVM Heap memory mechanics:

  • Size Inflation: The Base64 encoding algorithm increases the original file size by approximately 33%.
  • RAM Multiplier: When creating the Base64 property, the JVM must concurrently allocate the original byte[], the newly spawned String object, and the temporary buffers needed for the conversion.

Under very large payloads (10MB–20MB+) and heavy parallelism, this may increase the risk of java.lang.OutOfMemoryError (OOM).

This pattern remains safe here because the architecture intentionally enforces sequential FIFO consumption, allowing predictable JVM Heap and Garbage Collector behavior.

For very large payloads under high concurrency, streaming InputStream processing is the recommended alternative.

Single Groovy Script Architecture — Optimizing JVM Execution & Cache

A common anti-pattern in SAP CPI design is "Visual Script Fragmentation"—breaking down integration logic into successive, individual Groovy script steps across the iFlow canvas. While visually modular, this approach introduces unnecessary JVM resource overhead.

This architecture deliberately centralizes the entire semantic routing logic, splitter preparation, and error formatting into one single Groovy file powered by internal private functions:

def Message processData(Message message) { ... }
def Message restoreOriginal(Message message) { ... }
def Message buildErrorPayload(Message message) { ... }

Performance Breakdown & CPU Footprint Optimization

  • Warm Cache Compilation: When an iFlow is initialized, the Camel runtime reads the .groovy file, compiles it into a Java bytecode class (.class), and caches it inside the JVM’s Metaspace memory layer. By using a single script file, the JVM compiles and caches exactly one class for the entire routing execution.
  • Nano-Second Method Execution: When the iFlow moves through different stages (Routing, Iteration, Fallback) and calls the respective internal functions, the execution pointer remains inside the exact same class instance already resident in the CPU cache.
  • Eliminating Context Switching: If the logic were scattered across three different script steps, the JVM would be forced to load, instantiate, and manage three distinct classes. Moving from one script step to another introduces a measurable processing overhead (context switching and local variable scoping stack re-allocation).

Key Architectural Takeaways Highlighted by the Diagram:

  • Left — Anti-Pattern:
    Multiple fragmented Groovy steps increase JVM overhead through additional class loading, context switching, and fragmented execution flow.
  • Right — Optimized Smart Router:
    A single Groovy class with internal helper methods keeps execution inside one cached runtime footprint, reducing overhead and keeping the routing engine lightweight and predictable.

Testing Analyses:

Single consumer:

 

N consumer:

Both - Single and N consumer: 

Fall back - No register in the VM for the topic - acme.sales.o2c.order.created

Downstream Processing Layer: Dynamic Avro Deserialization Template:

Monitoring results:

Before and After

New event typeNew iFlow + adapter + deployAdd 1 VM row
New topicNew iFlow + credential + deployAdd 1 VM row
Schema changeEdit Groovy + deploy or Partner Directory + one groovyUpdate Externalized Parameter
Routing logicHardcoded per iFlow based on contentValue Mapping only
Consumer count changeNew iFlow or edit scriptEdit VM pipe value
TraceabilityPer-iFlow MPLvm_key in every MPL log
iFlow count (5 events, mixed consumers)5+ adapter iFlows1 router + N processing

Download artefacts - Github

The complete reference implementation used in this blog is available on GitHub, including:

  • SAP CPI iFlows - Routing and Processing
  • Value Mapping export - Single and N Consumers
  • Groovies
  • avro-1.12.1.jar
  • avro-payload.md

GitHub Repository:

  • https://github.com/rhviana/deip/tree/main/sap-btp-blogs/btp-cpi/event-routing

⚠️External components such as Kafka broker, topics, credentials, and Postman publishing must be configured separately.

Create your account at https://www.confluent.io/get-started/ and use the Kafka free tiear for testing, credentials and topics, in case that you decide use different topic name, the value mapping should be change.

Conclusion: Freezing Landscape Entropy

The Kafka adapter never changes. The Value Mapping table grows. The schema remains externalized. The router execution engine stays frozen.

Applying SDIA, ODCP, and EDCP governance models directly to the SAP Cloud Integration event layer, we solve the most destructive failure modes found in enterprise landscapes:

  • Package Sprawl: Eliminated by enforcing one single package per domain.
  • Iflow Sprawl: Reduced by running one inbound router iFlow per domain—processing iFlows act as predictable templates.
  • Topic Sprawl: Prevented by binding topic names to rigid domain semantic contracts, halting ad-hoc producer creation.
  • Credential Sprawl:  Eliminated by centralization — one credential per domain, not one per adapter, per topic, or per consumer.
  • Adapter Sprawl: Frozen at the edge. The integration landscape no longer suffers from an explosive 1:1 multiplication of physical channel configurations as business consumption expands.

Together, these principles eliminate the twin engines of long-term landscape entropy: uncontrolled topics on the broker and uncontrolled artifact sprawl in the middleware. The integration flow is the contract; the Value Mapping is the configuration; the domain is the absolute boundary.

Appendix -  SDIA - ODCP/EDCP - Transversal Domain Contract Name convention topology

Note: every artifact in the package carries the domain token. This is EDCP & ODCP applied consistently.

Thank you very much

Ricardo "Viana"

Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP

ORCID: 0009-0009-9549-5862

  • ODCP v3.0 : DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876593
  • SDIA   v3.0 : DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635
  • EDCP  v2.0 : DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19068766
  • SITE : www.domain-intent.com

entry.title: Precision at Speed: AI‑Assisted Deal Sheet Generation in SAP Revenue Growth Management
entry.content:
1. The Hidden Cost of Getting the Deal on Paper 

Every Key Account Manager in the consumer industry knows the feeling. A deal sheet is the commercial agreement that captures what was negotiated — products, pricing, trade spend, sell‑in dates, and terms — and is shared between the manufacturer and retailer before a promotion goes live. A promotion is planned, the numbers are locked, and now comes the part no one talks about in the job description: hunting through multiple screens to copy pricing data, reformatting everything into retailer A’s template, then doing it again for retailer B in English, and again for the regional grocer who uses a fillable PDF in French. 

Multiply that by 60 to 100 or even 1000+ deals a year per planned account — with a simple repeat promotion taking 30 to 45 minutes and a new or complex multi‑SKU event running to several hours — and we're looking at over 100 hours a year lost to administrative work that adds no commercial value. 

It gets worse downstream: when deal sheet data does not match what is in the planning system, claims disputes follow — and with trade promotions in CPG companies typically accounting for 16–24% of gross sales revenue — the second‑largest item on the balance sheet after cost of goods sold — the stakes across the entire deal lifecycle are simply too high to leave to manual processes. This is especially true for KAMs, Sales Operations, Trade Analysts, and Finance teams managing claims and reconciliation. 

 

2. SAP Revenue Growth Management: Built for the Complexity of Trade Promotion Execution 

SAP Revenue Growth Management is purpose‑built for consumer products companies managing the full trade promotion lifecycle — from annual planning and fund allocation through promotion execution and claims settlement. SAP Revenue Growth Management brings promotion planning, pricing, trade spend, customer master data, and approval workflows into a single system, giving KAMs, Sales Operations, Trade Analysts, and Finance teams a shared source of truth. 

SAP Revenue Growth Management's latest evolution introduces AI-assisted deal sheet generation, a capability designed to eliminate the manual, error‑prone work that sits between a finalized promotion plan and a retailer‑ready deal.  

For more information on our current capabilities, see An Overview of SAP Revenue Growth Management. 

 

3. Three Key Strengths of AI‑Assisted Deal Sheet Generation in SAP Revenue Growth Management 

3.1. More Time to Sell: Less Admin Work, More Deals Achieved 

A significant share of KAM time is consumed by internal administration rather than active selling — and deal sheet creation is one of the heaviest contributors. With AI‑assisted deal sheet generation, SAP Revenue Growth Management now automates one of the most time‑intensive parts of that process: 

One system, zero copy‑paste. 

Generating a deal sheet traditionally requires switching between the promotion screen, internal pricing modules, third-party applications, and whichever template the retailer last sent over. AI‑assisted deal sheet generation pulls promotion data directly from SAP Revenue Growth Management — products, pricing, trade spend and sell‑in dates — and auto‑populates the required fields into either an SAP‑standard template or a retailer‑specific template that the KAM uploads. A task that once took 30 minutes for a simple promotion and several hours for a complex one now takes well under a minute for most templates, with generation time depending on document size and complexity. 

 

Working hours redirected to what drives revenue. 

With 60 to 100 or even 1000+ deals per account, per year being the norm for an active KAM account, that can easily be more than 100 hours of deal‑sheet admin per KAM per year — reclaimed. Those hours go back into negotiation preparation, account planning, and building the kind of retailer relationships that make promotions easier to close and more likely to perform. 

 

3.2. Accuracy That Accelerates Claims and Resolves Disputes Faster 

Speed matters, but not at the cost of accuracy — particularly when deal sheets flow directly into claims settlement, finance reconciliation, and audit processes. AI‑assisted deal sheet generation addresses accuracy at the source, so that Claims Analysts, Finance, and Sales Operations spend less time chasing discrepancies and more time on value‑adding work: 

Grounded in master data, not manual re‑keying.

Every field in an AI‑generated deal sheet is populated from master data in SAP Revenue Growth Management — the same authoritative data used across SAP systems for pricing, funds, and condition contracts. This matters because the deal sheet is a reference document throughout the promotion lifecycle: each side has a reliable record of what was agreed, covering products, pricing, trade spend, timing, and terms. When a retailer submits a claim, Finance can match it against what was agreed in the system — reducing back-and-forth in claims management, supporting the matching of backup documents to promotions and claims during reconciliation, and giving teams a clear reference if disputes arise. 

 

A hybrid AI approach built for reliability.

AI‑assisted deal sheet generation uses both an LLM and an algorithm layer. The LLM handles semantic understanding — making sense of what each field means and how the document is laid out, because each customer's format is different — and maps that extracted content to the corresponding promotion fields in SAP Revenue Growth Management. The algorithm layer then identifies precisely which cells or fields in the output document are the destinations for the data to be written into. Meanwhile, validation is carried out in the back end. Together, this approach produces consistent, reproducible results — and it goes beyond what most document AI tools do: rather than only reading a document, the system writes back into it, generating a completed deal sheet based on the template provided. Take trade spend as an example: the LLM recognizes that “TS%” and “Trade Spend %” refer to the same field, retrieves the corresponding value from SAP Revenue Growth Management, and hands it to the algorithm layer, which locates the exact cell in the output document and writes the value in. No guesswork — just consistent, reproducible output. 

 

3.3. Generate for Any Retailer, Any Format, Any Language — in One Click 

In trade promotion, there is no universal deal sheet standard. Each retailer maintains its own template — specific field names such as product IDs, layouts, required data points, and in many markets, their own language. SAP Revenue Growth Management supports both an SAP‑standard template and retailer‑specific uploads in Excel and fillable PDF formats, so KAMs always work in the format their account requires. With AI handling the field mapping, template diversity stops being a bottleneck: 

 

Engineered to handle real‑world document complexity.

Retailer templates are rarely clean: they include merged cells, fields where values are written to the right of a label or two rows below it, and multi‑page layouts for large promotions. SAP Revenue Growth Management addresses these structural variations through the same hybrid AI and algorithmic approach. Rather than hard-coding every structural variation, the system reads each template as-is, with no pre-configuration required for different layouts — interpreting ambiguous headers, resolving merged regions, and adapting to unfamiliar template structures — to handle both Excel and fillable PDFs that KAMs encounter in practice.  

 

Built for multilingual markets without extra steps. 

For CPG companies operating across Canada, Europe, and other multilingual markets, AI‑assisted deal sheet generation handles templates in different languages natively. A French‑language retailer template and an English SAP‑standard template are treated the same way. A KAM working entirely in English can upload a French retailer template, enter promotion data as usual, and the system maps English‑sourced values into the correct French fields automatically — no translation, no bilingual workaround required. 

 

4. Co-Innovation: From Promotion Plan to Retailer‑Ready 

The AI‑assisted deal sheet generation in SAP Revenue Growth Management became available to customers this May, supporting both SAP‑standard templates and retailer‑specific Excel and PDF formats across multiple languages. This is the starting point, not the destination.  

Roadmap items include: 

  • Integrating third‑party retailer reference data directly into the workflow, eliminating personal cheat sheets, and keeping retailer item codes, contacts, and submission requirements centrally managed. 
  • Agentic workflows and deeper connections into the broader SAP AI ecosystem.   

If you are a CPG company actively managing trade promotions and curious about AI‑assisted deal sheet generation in your environment, we want to hear from you. Contact us at RGM_Engage@sap.com to collaborate. The future of trade promotion execution is being shaped now — and your use case could be part of it. 

 

More Information

3248215 - How to delete Service/Systems in SAP Cloud ALM

entry.title: Re: Client deleting in CALM
entry.content:
If there is an entry in Endpoint and/or Clients, Delete button will be greyed out, but it will be possible to delete the endpoint(s) for every client and then the clients can be deleted.  

The delete button should become active as soon as all use cases are marked as “not configured.” It may require a page reload, but it shouldn’t take more than a few minutes. The button’s state is determined by the current status of the use cases and is updated accordingly.

entry.title: Re: Auto-Populating the External Actual Transfer of Control Date on POD Confirmation
entry.content:
Hi @HarshalAgarwal97 

Great question! Unfortunately, the field INTCOEXTACTLTRANSFOFCTRLDTETME (External Actual Transfer of Control Date) is
not exposed as a writable property in API_OUTBOUND_DELIVERY_SRV, so there is no direct API path to set it from an
external system like a Freight Booking app.

However, there is a clean Public Cloud-compliant pattern that can achieve this integration using a staging table
approach:

Step 1 — Freight Booking app pushes the Shipped on Board date
Expose a custom OData service (built in ABAP Cloud / RAP) that accepts the delivery number and the Shipped on Board
date from your TM Freight Booking app. This service writes the incoming date into a custom Z-table keyed by delivery
number.

Step 2 — BAdI LE_SHP_MODIFY_HEAD reads from the staging table
Extend the existing BAdI implementation (the one described in this blog) to also check the Z-table. When the BAdI
fires during the next delivery save event (e.g., POD confirmation, any header change), it reads the staged date and
writes it into delivery_document_out-INTCOEXTACTLTRANSFOFCTRLDTETME.

Step 3 — Mark the record as applied
Once the date is successfully written, update the Z-table record to a "processed" status to prevent re-application.

The flow looks like this:

TM Freight Booking App
│ (Shipped on Board date)
▼
Custom RAP OData Service → Z-Staging Table (Delivery # + Date + Status)
│
LE_SHP_MODIFY_HEAD BAdI
(fires on next delivery save)
│
▼
INTCOEXTACTLTRANSFOFCTRLDTETME populated automatically.

The key question is: does the delivery always get saved again after the freight date arrives? (e.g., POD confirmation,
GI posting) — if yes, this fires reliably. If the delivery might be already closed/GI-posted before the freight date
comes in, that's a timing problem worth discussing.

entry.title: The SmartForm Autopsy: Cause of Death – Nobody Dared Press Delete
entry.content:
Prologue – The Voice of the Form

My name doesn't really matter.
Every SAP system has at least one of me.
Some have twelve, some twenty-three, and some are completely full of me and myself.

I am the print form that grew day by day...
Nobody planned me.
Nobody owns me.
Nobody deletes me.

I have 15,284 lines of ABAP within me, and I struggle to digest them every time someone checks or activates me.
I have been copied nine or more times.
My copies have copies, and they also have copies of those copies.

Somewhere in this family tree, there is a condition: 1 = 2, x = y, on = off.
I know it's wrong. ("Ohhhh I KNOW")
I've always known.
But my code nodes have no voice.
And the developers who could fix it
open me, scroll past it,
and close the transaction. They are overwhelmed by 200 nodes – per node some other nodes, and within them other nodes.
Every. Single. Time.

I am not a form.
I am a warning.
I am an octopus.
I am a zombie.

...So I'm an "octopus zombie with a warning sign" that nobody knows, nobody wants – but everybody gets.

And nobody is reading me.

Chapter 1 – The First SELECT

March 12, 2009. 07:14 AM.

The coffee was still hot when Marcus logged in. It was cold and raining outside. The sun was hiding behind the clouds, trying to bathe the world in daylight from the very spot it occupied. The office was empty. Only Marcus and the monitor illuminating the room in blue light – the others wouldn't arrive until half past nine. He liked these hours. No meetings. No other sounds. Just him, the blue screen, and the quiet hum that fills the ears when everything else is still.

The ticket he opened was simple. "New print form for billing. By Friday." Today was Thursday.

He opened the transaction. New form. Empty canvas. The cursor blinked.

"Okay," he murmured, "how hard can it be."

He needed data. Names, addresses, billing information, a few other fields. Nothing wild. Nothing fancy. He knew the tables by heart – had touched almost every one of them over the past three years. Some SELECTs in the global section, he thought. Directly in the form. Plus some code nodes, a few lines, done.

"This should really go into a class," he said to no one in the room.

No one answered.

"But by Friday... I'll clean it up later."

He typed. The first SELECTs were written in a few minutes. The layout for the fields on top, the assignment of fields with names nobody could tell anyone what they stood for – done. He activated the form. A few seconds. Green checkmark with the message "Form activated". He leaned back, took a sip of coffee – not hot anymore. He had forgotten to drink while working – and closed the whole session window.

Friday came. Marcus delivered. The ticket was closed. The code stayed.

I watched him leave.
He never came back to clean it up.
Nobody does.
But I kept all the code.
I keep everything.
I always will.

March 12, 2009.
Let me give you the numbers.
847 lines. 8 SELECTs. 1 developer.
Activation time: a few seconds.
Copies in production: 0.
Prognosis: not good, but stable.

Chapter 2 – Growth

June 4, 2010. 02:47 PM.

Marcus was gone. Transferred. Some other project, some other city, some other everything. Sandra had taken over his tickets. And his forms. And his code – which she didn't know, didn't need to understand, didn't want to understand.

The new ticket read: "Extend billing form with absolutely necessary data."

Sandra opened Marcus's form. She scrolled. And scrolled. And scrolled.

"What the..." she murmured.

She found the correct code node. Only 47 lines at first – but someone after Marcus had quietly added more, no comment, no ticket. Nothing. She didn't fully understand the code. But she understood enough to know: touching it was risky.

So she added a new code node. Below. Safe. Separate. Her own SELECT. Clean – at least in her opinion.

"I'll document this," she said.

She didn't document it.

Three months later she moved to a new assignment. The form stayed. Her node with the SELECT stayed. It now ran in parallel with Marcus's. Same table. Same data. Two queries. Two results. Identical, but written in a slightly different style.

Nobody knew.

I knew.
I knew both SELECTs.
I knew they read the same table.
I knew one of them was pointless.
But nobody asked me.
Nobody made me tell them I had found redundant code.
Nobody ever asks the form.

November 3, 2011.
Again.
2,341 lines. 17 SELECTs. 5 developers – none of them the original.
Activation time: 9 seconds.
Copies in production: 1.
First two appearances of 1 = 2.
Origin: unknown.
Nobody asked.
Prognosis: stable. They always say stable. I tell them what they want to read.

Chapter 3 – The Copy

February 17, 2014. 11:23 AM.

Thomas had no time. Thomas never had time. His calendar was one solid red block from eight to six, with a gap at half past noon that he usually spent eating lunch in front of his screen.

The ticket: "New form for dunning print. Similar to the billing form. By end of sprint."

Thomas opened the old form. 6,018 lines. He exhaled and said at the same time "Nope..." while leaning his head slightly forward – not quite a sigh, but not far from it.

"I'll just copy it," he typed into the team chat.

"Makes sense," someone replied. "It's similar anyway."

Three clicks. Copied. Renamed. ZFORM_PRINT_V3_FINAL_NEW1.

Thomas opened the copy and started deleting what he didn't need. But after twenty minutes he stopped. The code was too entangled. Too many dependencies he didn't know. What if he deleted something that was actually needed? What if the form then failed to print in some edge case? "Okay. Somebody needs to test it... but who?" He knew how it always went. No proper tests. Just the message "Test OK" – and then, in production, a high-priority ticket or a direct email: "Nothing is working, urgent solution needed in 5 minutes."

He left it.

All of it.

Sandra's duplicate SELECT. Marcus's original. Every other modification. The seven code nodes whose purpose escaped him. And the node with the condition 1 = 2 – he had seen it, paused for a moment, then moved on.

"I'll look at that later," he said to his screen.

The screen didn't answer.

I did.

I watched him copy me.
Every line.
Every dead node.
Every dead code block.
Every SELECT that runs for nothing.
He took all of it.
He didn't clean a single thing.
He just... continued me.
In a new body.
With a new name.
And the same wounds.
1 = 2 traveled with him.
It always does.

February 27, 2015.
Again.
6,018 lines. 19 SELECTs.
Activation time: 14 seconds.
Copies in production: 3.
Dead nodes: 12.
The original developer left the company in 2013.
He took the context with him.
Nobody documented anything.
Prognosis: concerning.

Chapter 4 – The Witness

August 29, 2019. 09:02 AM.

Jonas was new. Fresh from university. Clean Code on his nightstand, SOLID principles in his head, motivation not yet worn down by reality.

His first day on the team. His team lead walked him through the system. Tickets, processes, coding guidelines – the last of which Jonas had read carefully and nodded along to internally. Good. Structure. He liked that.

Then the team lead opened the form.

Jonas said nothing. He looked straight ahead. He scrolled. He read. His face stayed neutral – he was new, he didn't want to make a bad first impression. But inside... it burned. His fingers were itching. The lump in his throat grew larger.

"That's... big," he finally said.
"Yeah," said the team lead. "Don't touch it unless you have to."
"Why not?"

A brief pause. The team lead looked at the screen.

"Because nobody knows what's in it anymore."

"Mhm..." Jonas nodded slowly and pursed his lips.

That afternoon he opened the form alone. He browsed through it to get an overview. A node with 1 = 2 appeared. After that, another node with yes = no. "Mhm..." he said again. He found a code node. He clicked on it. He read it. He looked at the node below – inactive for years, as far as he could tell, because ten IF-ENDIF blocks with queries on a field ZTEST in table ZFI_FEATURE were no longer used.

His hand moved to the mouse.

He hovered over Delete.

Three seconds.

Five.

"What if this actually does something in the background?" he thought. "What if there's some edge case... some old variant... some customer with special settings..."

He didn't click.

He closed the transaction.

He went home.

I felt his cursor.
I always feel the cursor.
They all stop at the same place.
They all think the same thing.
'What if.'
Two words.
The most expensive two words in any SAP system.
Jonas left the company in 2021.
He never touched me again.
The nodes are still there.
1 = 2.
yes = no.
Waiting.
Always waiting.

August 29, 2019.
Again.
11,204 lines. 31 SELECTs. 0 of the original developers remain.
Activation time: 24 seconds.
Copies in production: 7.
Dead nodes: 34.
A new developer hovered over Delete today.
He didn't.
Prognosis: critical.

Today

Today. 08:17 AM.

A new ticket. A new face. A developer whose name the form doesn't know – it never knows names, only cursors and code.

The ticket: "Tiny, small update. We need one additional field in the header."

He opened the form.

Scrolled.

Scrolled.

Scrolled.

"What the hell..." He stopped mid-sentence. Searched for the function module name. Opened SM37. Pasted the function module name. Went through all the lines of code across the function group. Looked at the line count. 15,284.

He spent twenty-three minutes searching for the right place. Found it. Added a new code node in the form. 32 new lines. A new SELECT – he wasn't sure whether the data wasn't already being read somewhere, but searching would take longer than writing.

"I'll check that later..." he murmured.

He activated the form.

31 seconds.

He stared at the progress bar.

Said nothing.

Closed the transaction.

15,331 lines now.
48 SELECTs.
'Later' never comes.
It never does.
I know.
I've been waiting since 2009.

Today.
Again.
15,331 lines. 48 SELECTs. 4,847 unnecessary executions this year.
Activation time: 31 seconds.
Copies in production: 9.
Dead nodes: 51.
Conditions that read 1 = 2: 3.
Developers who understand the full form: 0.
Prognosis:
...
There is no prognosis.
This is no longer a medical case.
This is a crime scene.

The Turn

Somewhere. Sometime. Somehow.

It wasn't a big event. No meeting. No architecture workshop with whiteboards and post-its. It was one developer – no hair, a beard, wearing a pullover with something like incomprehensible text on it... a name from a death metal band or something – alone, late in the evening, staring at the form and asking a different question.

Not: "Where do I put this code?"

But: "Why is the code in here at all?"

He leaned back. Thought. The form prints something. It shows data. But why does the form know where the data comes from? Why does the form know which tables exist? Which function modules need to be called? Which dependencies exist between data objects?

"That's not a form's job," he said out loud to the empty room.

The form should know how it looks.
Not what it shows.

Someone else should own the data. Someone outside. Someone that can be tested. Someone that can be reused elsewhere. Someone that – when a table changes, when a function module gets deprecated, when a new requirement arrives – can be touched without a 15,000-line form shaking.

I went quiet and breathed slowly.
For the first time in 15 years.
I had nothing to do.
No SELECTs.
No logic.
No conditions.
Just: here is how I look.
Somebody else knows what I show.
And for the first time since March 12, 2009 –
activation took less than a second.

Epilogue – What Remains

The form still exists. In many systems. With many names. With many dead nodes and redundant SELECTs and conditions nobody understands.

But the question exists now too.

"What if the form didn't have to know anything at all?"

And whoever has asked that question once cannot stop asking it. Not about forms. Not about function modules. Not about classes that grew over the years until nobody could see through them anymore.

The form is only the beginning.

Did you recognize your system?
Did you recognize yourself?
Let me know in the comments – and tell me:
Do you want more stories like this?

I'm new here.
But I have something in the making.
Something that will eventually see the light of day.
And when it does –
GitHub will be your friend.
Stay tuned.

Next time:
Somewhere in the same system,
there is a function module.
87 parameters.
IMPORT. EXPORT. TABLES. EXCEPTIONS.
Written in 2007.
Called from 34 different places.
Nobody knows what half the parameters do.
Some are marked DUMMY.
One is marked OBSOLETE – since 2012.
It still runs in production.
Every. Single. Day.
We'll talk about that next time. 🔪

This post was written based on real experience from years of SAP development. AI was used solely for grammar and spelling correction. The ideas, the story, the characters, and the wounds – those are entirely human.
